(often denoted by **τ**, [Greek](/wiki/Greek_alphabet "Greek alphabet"): [tau](/wiki/Tau "Tau")) is the component of [stress](/wiki/Stress_(physics) "Stress (physics)") [coplanar](/wiki/Coplanar "Coplanar") with a [material cross section](/wiki/Material_cross_section "Material cross section"). It arises from the [shear force](/wiki/Shear_force "Shear force"), the component of [force](/wiki/Force "Force") vector parallel to the material cross section. *[Normal stress](/wiki/Normal_stress "Normal stress")*, on the other hand, arises from the force vector component perpendicular to the material cross section on which it acts.

expresses the retarding force (per unit area) from a wall in the layers of a fluid flowing next to the wall. It is defined as:![{\displaystyle \tau _{w}:=\mu \left.{\frac {\partial u}{\partial y}}\right|_{y=0},}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/e5c7853319e2f8e971f5bf23c416319a830a086e)where μ is the [dynamic viscosity](/wiki/Dynamic_viscosity "Dynamic viscosity"), u is the flow velocity, and y is the distance from the wall.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Component of stress coplanar with a material cross section

| Shear stress | |
| --- | --- |
| Common symbols | [τ](/wiki/Tau "Tau") |
| [SI unit](/wiki/SI_unit "SI unit") | [pascal](/wiki/Pascal_(unit) "Pascal (unit)") |
| Derivations from other quantities | *τ* = ⁠*F*/*A*⁠ |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/Shear_stress_simple.svg/250px-Shear_stress_simple.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Shear_stress_simple.svg)

Side view of a [parallelepiped](/wiki/Parallelepiped "Parallelepiped") where a shearing force is applied to the top of a [rectangular cuboid](/wiki/Rectangular_cuboid "Rectangular cuboid") while the bottom is held in place. The resulting shear stress, τ, deforms the cuboid's rectangular side into a [parallelogram](/wiki/Parallelogram "Parallelogram"). The area involved would be the top of the parallelopiped.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/Shear_scherung.svg/250px-Shear_scherung.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Shear_scherung.svg)

Perspective view of a [parallelepiped](/wiki/Parallelepiped "Parallelepiped") where a shearing force F is applied to the top of a [rectangular cuboid](/wiki/Rectangular_cuboid "Rectangular cuboid") with area A while the bottom is held in place. The resulting shear stress deforms the cuboid's rectangular side into a [parallelogram](/wiki/Parallelogram "Parallelogram").

**Shear stress** (often denoted by **τ**, [Greek](/wiki/Greek_alphabet "Greek alphabet"): [tau](/wiki/Tau "Tau")) is the component of [stress](/wiki/Stress_(physics) "Stress (physics)") [coplanar](/wiki/Coplanar "Coplanar") with a [material cross section](/wiki/Material_cross_section "Material cross section"). It arises from the [shear force](/wiki/Shear_force "Shear force"), the component of [force](/wiki/Force "Force") vector parallel to the material cross section. *[Normal stress](/wiki/Normal_stress "Normal stress")*, on the other hand, arises from the force vector component perpendicular to the material cross section on which it acts.

## General shear stress

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shear_stress&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: General shear stress")]

The formula to calculate average shear stress τ or force per unit area is[[1]](#cite_note-1)
![{\displaystyle \tau ={F \over A},}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/e1dc96a913ad2104c3de14f8e93d2b865f4b109b)where F is the force applied and A is the cross-sectional area.

## Other forms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shear_stress&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Other forms")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Shear_stress "Special:EditPage/Shear stress") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners") in this section. Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *(September 2022)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

### Wall shear stress

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shear_stress&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Wall shear stress")]

**Wall shear stress** expresses the retarding force (per unit area) from a wall in the layers of a fluid flowing next to the wall. It is defined as:![{\displaystyle \tau _{w}:=\mu \left.{\frac {\partial u}{\partial y}}\right|_{y=0},}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/e5c7853319e2f8e971f5bf23c416319a830a086e)where μ is the [dynamic viscosity](/wiki/Dynamic_viscosity "Dynamic viscosity"), u is the flow velocity, and y is the distance from the wall.

It is used, for example, in the description of arterial [blood flow](/wiki/Blood_flow "Blood flow"), where there is evidence that it affects the [atherogenic](/wiki/Atherogenic "Atherogenic") process.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

### Pure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shear_stress&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Pure")]

[Pure shear](/wiki/Pure_shear "Pure shear") stress is related to pure [shear strain](/wiki/Shear_strain "Shear strain"), denoted γ, by the equation[[3]](#cite_note-3)![{\displaystyle \tau =\gamma G,}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/4d5d448f7e810d6628ef10f9558f9c6ec472089e)where G is the [shear modulus](/wiki/Shear_modulus "Shear modulus") of the [isotropic](/wiki/Isotropic#Materials_science "Isotropic") material, given by![{\displaystyle G={\frac {E}{2(1+\nu )}}.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/d5f76ea386b45e41bcf99542f6a39a67f2a276f7)Here, E is [Young's modulus](/wiki/Young%27s_modulus "Young's modulus") and ν is [Poisson's ratio](/wiki/Poisson%27s_ratio "Poisson's ratio").

### Beam shear

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shear_stress&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Beam shear")]

Beam shear is defined as the internal shear stress of a beam caused by the shear force applied to the beam:![{\displaystyle \tau :={\frac {fQ}{Ib}},}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/4d55e03907f2d8b876e5d2932d31e5d50b4576fa)where

* f is the total shear force at the location in question,
* Q is the [statical moment of area](/wiki/First_moment_of_area#Statical_moment_of_area "First moment of area"),
* b is the thickness (width) in the material perpendicular to the shear, and
* I is the [moment of inertia](/wiki/Second_moment_of_area "Second moment of area") of the entire cross-sectional area.

The beam shear formula is also known as Zhuravskii shear stress formula after [Dmitrii Ivanovich Zhuravskii](/wiki/Dmitrii_Ivanovich_Zhuravskii "Dmitrii Ivanovich Zhuravskii"), who derived it in 1855.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

### Semi-monocoque shear

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shear_stress&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Semi-monocoque shear")]

Further information: [Shear flow](/wiki/Shear_flow "Shear flow")

Shear stresses within a [semi-monocoque](/wiki/Semi-monocoque "Semi-monocoque") structure may be calculated by idealizing the cross-section of the structure into a set of stringers (carrying only axial loads) and webs (carrying only [shear flows](/wiki/Shear_flow "Shear flow")). Dividing the shear flow by the thickness of a given portion of the semi-monocoque structure yields the shear stress. Thus, the maximum shear stress will occur either in the web of maximum shear flow or minimum thickness.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Constructions in soil can also fail due to shear; [e.g.](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/e.g. "wiktionary:e.g."), the weight of an earth-filled [dam](/wiki/Dam "Dam") or [dike](/wiki/Dyke_(construction) "Dyke (construction)") may cause the subsoil to collapse, like a small [landslide](/wiki/Landslide "Landslide").

### Impact shear

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shear_stress&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Impact shear")]

The maximum shear stress created in a solid round bar subject to impact is given by the equation[[6]](#cite_note-6)![{\displaystyle \tau =2{\sqrt {\frac {UG}{V}}},}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/42b03cc46978be7ea9acdcf183734acb2d736e2b)where

* U is the change in kinetic energy,
* G is the [shear modulus](/wiki/Shear_modulus "Shear modulus"), and
* V is the volume of the rod.

Furthermore,

*U* = *U*rotating + *U*applied,

where

* *U*rotating = ⁠1/2⁠*Iω*2,
* *U*applied = *Tθ*displaced,
* I is the mass moment of inertia, and
* ω is the angular speed.

### Shear stress in fluids

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shear_stress&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Shear stress in fluids")]

See also: [Viscosity](/wiki/Viscosity "Viscosity"), [Couette flow](/wiki/Couette_flow "Couette flow"), [Hagen-Poiseuille equation](/wiki/Hagen-Poiseuille_equation "Hagen-Poiseuille equation"), [Depth-slope product](/wiki/Depth-slope_product "Depth-slope product"), [Simple shear](/wiki/Simple_shear "Simple shear"), and [Wind stress](/wiki/Wind_stress "Wind stress")

[![Laminar shear in Couette Flow](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/Laminar_shear.svg/250px-Laminar_shear.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Laminar_shear.svg)

[Couette flow](/wiki/Couette_flow "Couette flow") is frequently used to illustrate [shear-driven](/wiki/Shearing_(physics) "Shearing (physics)") fluid motion.

Any real [fluids](/wiki/Fluids "Fluids") ([liquids](/wiki/Liquid "Liquid") and [gases](/wiki/Gas "Gas") included) moving along a solid boundary will incur a shear stress at that boundary. The [no-slip condition](/wiki/No-slip_condition "No-slip condition")[[7]](#cite_note-7) dictates that the speed of the fluid at the boundary (relative to the boundary) is zero, although at some height from the boundary, the flow speed must equal that of the fluid. The region between these two points is named the [boundary layer](/wiki/Boundary_layer "Boundary layer"). For all [Newtonian fluids](/wiki/Newtonian_fluid "Newtonian fluid") in [laminar flow](/wiki/Laminar_flow "Laminar flow"), the shear stress is proportional to the [strain rate](/wiki/Strain_rate "Strain rate") in the fluid, where the viscosity is the constant of proportionality. For [non-Newtonian fluids](/wiki/Non-Newtonian_fluid "Non-Newtonian fluid"), the [viscosity](/wiki/Viscosity "Viscosity") is not constant. The shear stress is imparted onto the boundary as a result of this loss of velocity.

For a Newtonian fluid, the shear stress at a surface element parallel to a flat plate at the point y is given by![{\displaystyle \tau (y)=\mu {\frac {\partial u}{\partial y}},}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/1773585aafe38ac7f235feceb13611ca828c0c1a)where

* μ is the [dynamic viscosity](/wiki/Dynamic_viscosity "Dynamic viscosity") of the flow,
* u is the [flow velocity](/wiki/Flow_velocity "Flow velocity") along the boundary, and
* y is the height above the boundary.

Specifically, the wall shear stress is defined as![{\displaystyle \tau _{\mathrm {w} }:=\tau (y=0)=\mu \left.{\frac {\partial u}{\partial y}}\right|_{y=0}~.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/0f3b07b978f7fde0f9a7379e64a62ef116b103f4)[Newton's constitutive law](/wiki/Newton%27s_law_of_viscosity "Newton's law of viscosity"), for any general geometry (including the flat plate above mentioned), states that shear tensor (a second-order tensor) is proportional to the flow velocity [gradient](/wiki/Gradient "Gradient") (the velocity is a vector, so its gradient is a second-order tensor):![{\displaystyle {\boldsymbol {\tau }}(\mathbf {u} )=\mu {\boldsymbol {\nabla }}\mathbf {u} .}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/9c522ecff60a67e5e8c83317e2f2caa2e5f04b89)The constant of proportionality is named *dynamic viscosity*. For an isotropic Newtonian flow, it is a scalar, while for anisotropic Newtonian flows, it can be a second-order tensor. The fundamental aspect is that for a Newtonian fluid, the dynamic viscosity is independent of flow velocity (i.e., the shear stress constitutive law is *linear*), while for non-Newtonian flows this is not true, and one should allow for the modification![{\displaystyle {\boldsymbol {\tau }}(\mathbf {u} )=\mu (\mathbf {u} ){\boldsymbol {\nabla }}\mathbf {u} .}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/e9ddcf89408acf6701097bd96cec878086f4c53c)This no longer Newton's law but a generic tensorial identity: one can always find an expression of the viscosity as function of the flow velocity given any expression of the shear stress as function of the flow velocity. On the other hand, given a shear stress as function of the flow velocity, it represents a Newtonian flow only if it can be expressed as a constant for the gradient of the flow velocity. The constant one finds in this case is the dynamic viscosity of the flow.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Measurement with sensors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shear_stress&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Measurement with sensors")]

### Diverging fringe shear stress sensor

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shear_stress&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Diverging fringe shear stress sensor")]

This relationship can be exploited to measure the wall shear stress. If a sensor could directly measure the gradient of the velocity profile at the wall, then multiplying by the dynamic viscosity would yield the shear stress. Such a sensor was demonstrated by A. A. Naqwi and W. C. Reynolds.[[8]](#cite_note-8) The interference pattern generated by sending a beam of light through two parallel slits forms a network of linearly diverging fringes that seem to originate from the plane of the two slits (see [double-slit experiment](/wiki/Double-slit_experiment "Double-slit experiment")). As a particle in a fluid passes through the fringes, a receiver detects the reflection of the fringe pattern. The signal can be processed, and from the fringe angle, the height and velocity of the particle can be extrapolated. The measured value of the wall velocity gradient is independent of the fluid properties, and as a result does not require calibration. Recent[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] advancements in the micro-optic fabrication technologies have made it possible to use integrated diffractive optical elements to fabricate diverging fringe shear stress sensors usable both in air and liquid.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

### Micro-pillar shear-stress sensor

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shear_stress&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Micro-pillar shear-stress sensor")]

A further measurement technique is that of slender wall-mounted micro-pillars made of the flexible polymer [polydimethylsiloxane](/wiki/Polydimethylsiloxane "Polydimethylsiloxane"), which bend in reaction to the applying drag forces in the vicinity of the wall. The sensor thereby belongs to the indirect measurement principles relying on the relationship between near-wall velocity gradients and the local wall-shear stress.[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)

### Electro-diffusional method

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shear_stress&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Electro-diffusional method")]

The electro-diffusional method measures the wall shear rate in the liquid phase from microelectrodes under limiting diffusion current conditions. A potential difference between an anode of a broad surface (usually located far from the measuring area) and the small working electrode acting as a cathode leads to a fast redox reaction. The ion disappearance occurs only on the microprobe active surface, causing the development of the diffusion boundary layer, in which the fast electro-diffusion reaction rate is controlled only by diffusion. The resolution of the convective-diffusive equation in the near-wall region of the microelectrode lead to analytical solutions relying the characteristics length of the microprobes, the diffusional properties of the electrochemical solution, and the wall shear rate.[[12]](#cite_note-12)
